title: The Energetic Cost of Improved Acid Resistance
author_string: Keffeler, Erica Catherine, Payne, Sophie, Blum, Paul
abstract:
Thermoacidophiles require specific mechanisms in order to withstand extreme temperatures and low pH.
                    Sulfolobus solfataricus
                    is an aerobic acidophile that is native to acidic geothermal environments. The Blum lab adapted
                    S. solfataricus
                    to possess a unique, heritable super acid resistance trait with the capability to survive in a pH that is exceedingly lower than its optimum. These newly adapted lineages were named super acid resistant Crenarchaeota (SARC). Energy is required for acid resistance, but it is predicted that SARC strains require an excess amount of energy to resist an extremely low pH.
                    S. solfataricus
                    receives its energy through their central carbon metabolism by generating electron carriers that are transferred to the electron transport chain to produce ATP. In this work, the transcriptomic data derived from the SARC lineages was analyzed to produce potential explanations on how the SARC strain met their energy demands in a more acidic environment. In addition to this work, chromatin proteins are also an interest due to their potential contribution to the SARC phenotype. A novel 8kDa chromatin protein is suspected to play a role in acid resistance gene expression, therefore it will be expressed and purified for downstream x‐ray crystallization studies.
